name: Tsonga
description: The Tsonga (Vatsonga) inhabit Limpopo Province and extend into southern Mozambique and Zimbabwe. They include several related groups — Shangaan, Thonga, Ronga, Ndzawu — and are famous for high-energy dance (Muchongolo), bright woven fabrics, and intricate beadwork. The Tsonga were profoundly affected by the Nguni military expansion (Mfecane) when Zulu general Soshangane established the Gaza Kingdom over their territory in the 1820s; many Tsonga clans still bear Shangaan identity from this period.
cultural_highlights: Muchongolo warrior dance — a high-energy acrobatic war dance originally performed before raids; now a showpiece at cultural events
Xibelani skirt dance — women's circular skirt dance where layers of raffia and cloth create a spinning visual effect at ceremonies
Tilo (sky god) spiritual traditions — the Tsonga traditional religious system centres on ancestral spirits and the high god Tilo
Decorated homestead arts — Tsonga women create elaborate painted and sculpted decorations on their homestead walls

food_traditions.name: Xidzimba (pumpkin and maize)
food_traditions.description: Dried pumpkin slices cooked with maize — a preservation technique turned everyday comfort food

food_traditions.name: Masonja (mopane worms)
food_traditions.description: Dried or fried mopane caterpillars — a protein-rich Limpopo delicacy shared by Tsonga, Venda, and Pedi communities

food_traditions.name: Xigugu (dried meat)
food_traditions.description: Sun-dried beef or game meat — a traditional preservation method for the hot dry lowveld climate

music_traditions: Xitende bow — a mouth resonated musical bow played for personal music-making and courtship
Xizambi — a lamellophone (thumb piano) played in quiet moments and small social settings
Muchongolo drumming — a specific ensemble of drums and voices driving the warrior dance tradition
